full_text: Rhino Foods Rhino Foods Incorporated, founded in 1981 by Anne and Ted Castle, is a specialty ice cream novelty and ice cream ingredient manufacturer located in Burlington, Vermont, United States. Rhino Foods has grown from a small ice cream shop, Chessy's Frozen Custard, into a business with over 150 employees. In 2013, the company became a certified B Corporation joining over a 1000 companies committed to using business as a force for good. This status reflects Rhino Foods' dedication to high social and environmental performance, accountability, and transparency. The company is best known for its unique products, including cookie dough pieces and other mix-ins that are utilized by ice cream makers across the nation. Their passion for quality ingredients and sustainable practices has positioned them as a leader in the ice cream ingredient market. Rhino Foods prioritizes working with local suppliers to source raw materials, ensuring that their operations are environmentally friendly and support the local economy. In addition to their commitment to sustainability, Rhino Foods places a strong emphasis on community engagement. They often participate in local events, support food-related initiatives, and encourage their employees to volunteer. The company's commitment to social responsibility is mirrored in their workplace culture, where employee well-being is a top priority. Through initiatives like fair wages, comprehensive benefits, and flexible work schedules, Rhino Foods fosters a supportive environment that allows employees to thrive both personally and professionally. Their mission extends beyond just creating delicious ice cream ingredients; they aim to inspire others in the food industry to adopt similar values. As part of their ongoing efforts, Rhino Foods invests in research and development to innovate new flavors and products that cater to changing consumer preferences. With a focus on health-conscious options and alternative dietary needs, they are continually expanding their portfolio to include vegan and gluten-free offerings. With a strong foundation built on quality, community, and environmental responsibility, Rhino Foods is poised for continued growth in the specialty ice cream market while setting a standard for ethical business practices in the industry.

Jon Mitchell Jon Mitchell is a British meteorologist and weather presenter for "Calendar" and "Granada Reports". He grew up in Morecambe in Lancashire, attending Lancaster Grammar School (since 1971 Lancaster Grammar School). Jon Mitchell started work with the Met Office on leaving school in 1978. At that time he described work as: "We spent most of our time analyzing weather patterns and learning the intricacies of meteorological science. It was an exhilarating experience for a young enthusiast like myself." After several years of hands-on experience, Jon transitioned into broadcasting, where he could share his passion for weather with the public. His charismatic on-screen presence quickly made him a popular figure on various regional channels. Audiences appreciated his ability to break down complex weather data into relatable forecasts that everyone could understand. In addition to his television work, Jon was also keen to educate others about the science of weather. He began hosting community workshops and school visits, inspiring a new generation of meteorologists. "It's important to foster curiosity about the natural world," he often said. "Understanding the weather helps us prepare for the future and appreciate the environment we live in." As his career progressed, Jon became known not just for his accurate forecasts but also for his commitment to environmental issues. He frequently addressed climate change and its impact on local weather patterns during his broadcasts. His segments on "Granada Reports" often included tips on how viewers could reduce their carbon footprint and engage in sustainable practices. Through his dedicated work and enthusiasm for meteorology, Jon Mitchell has left a lasting impact on both his viewers and the scientific community. Today, he continues to be a beloved figure in British broadcasting, bridging the gap between science and everyday life with each weather report he presents.

Run of the House Run of the House is an American sitcom television series created by Betsy Thomas, that aired on The WB between September 11, 2003 and May 7, 2004. Nineteen episodes were produced but only sixteen were aired before the show was cancelled. The show was about a family of four siblings, whose parents moved from Grand Rapids, Michigan, to a suburban neighborhood in California, leaving the children to manage the household on their own. The siblings, each with distinct personalities, navigated the ups and downs of adolescent life while trying to maintain order in their chaotic home. At the heart of the series were the eldest brother, a responsible yet often overwhelmed figure trying to keep his younger siblings in line; the rebellious middle sister who constantly challenged authority; the quirky brother with imaginative antics; and the free-spirited youngest sister who added a dose of innocence to their antics. Throughout the short-lived series, viewers watched as the siblings dealt with typical teenage dilemmas — from crushes and friendships to academic pressures and family dynamics. Although the humor often stemmed from their attempts to handle everyday challenges, there were deeper themes of love and loyalty that emerged. The show's clever writing, combined with its relatable characters, contributed to a charm that resonated with many fans. Despite its short run, "Run of the House" remains a nostalgic reminder of the trials of growing up and the importance of family support, even in the face of absurdity.

Kingdom of Sussex The Kingdom of the South Saxons, today referred to as the Kingdom of Sussex (; ), was one of the seven traditional kingdoms of the Heptarchy of Anglo-Saxon England. On the south coast of the island of Great Britain, Sussex was bordered by the English Channel to the south, with its fertile lands extending northward towards the hills of the South Downs. The kingdom's establishment dates back to the early 5th century, as waves of Anglo-Saxon settlers began to populate the area, bringing with them their own customs, language, and beliefs. The heart of Sussex was its royal center, which eventually became associated with Chichester, the capital of the kingdom. Here, the rulers of Sussex established their authority, often engaging in both trade and conflict with neighboring kingdoms such as Wessex, Kent, and Mercia. The kingdom's strategic position along the English coast also made it a vital point for maritime trade and defense against invasions. One of the notable aspects of Sussex was its vibrant culture, which blended the traditions of the Anglo-Saxons with local influences. The interconnectedness of the communities fostered a thriving economy based on agriculture, fishing, and trade. The fertile soil of the Downs supported the production of grain and livestock, while coastal access allowed for an abundant supply of fish. As Sussex evolved, so too did its political landscape. The kingdom experienced various shifts in power, including the introduction of Christianity in the late 6th century, which prompted the construction of churches that would serve as centers of worship and learning. The arrival of missionaries, such as St. Wilfrid, played a significant role in converting the local populace and integrating Sussex into the wider Christian community of England. Throughout its history, Sussex faced numerous threats, from Viking raids in the late 8th century to territorial disputes with its more powerful neighbors. Despite these challenges, the resilience of the South Saxons allowed them to maintain their identity and customs for centuries. The decline of Sussex as an independent kingdom began in the 9th century when it gradually fell under the influence of Wessex. By the 10th century, Sussex had become part of the larger kingdom of England as a result of unification efforts that aimed to create a more cohesive national identity. Today, the legacy of the Kingdom of Sussex can still be seen in the region's place names, historical sites, and cultural traditions. The rich history of the South Saxons continues to fascinate historians and enthusiasts alike, reminding us of the complex tapestry of England's early medieval past.

Things Ain't What They Used to Be (And You Better Believe It) Things Ain't What They Used to Be is a 1970 studio album by the American jazz singer Ella Fitzgerald - the final album that Fitzgerald recorded on the Reprise Records label. The album was re-issued on CD with alternative artwork in 1989. It was released as part of Fitzgerald's repertoire during a pivotal era in her career, showcasing her ability to adapt to the changing musical landscape of the time. The album features a collection of tracks that blend jazz with emerging influences from pop and rock, reflecting the dynamic shifts in popular music during the early 1970s. Notable songs on the album include the titular track, which explores themes of change and nostalgia, capturing a sense of longing for simpler times. Fitzgerald's signature vocal style shines through, imbued with a rich emotional depth that resonates with listeners. Other standout tracks highlight her remarkable versatility, as she effortlessly navigates different genres while maintaining her distinct sound. Critics and fans alike have praised the album for its innovative arrangements and the way it captures the essence of the era. Ella's ability to interpret contemporary songs with her unique flavor added a layer of authenticity, drawing in both old admirers and new fans. In addition to its musical content, the album's cover art also marked a moment of innovation, deliberately designed to catch the eye and reflect the boldness of both Fitzgerald and the music industry at the time. The 1989 re-issue introduced a fresh perspective, rekindling interest in this classic collection. Overall, Things Ain't What They Used to Be serves as a testament to Ella Fitzgerald's enduring legacy, illustrating her role as a groundbreaking artist who continually evolved without compromising her artistic identity. It stands as a significant entry in her discography, exemplifying the transition of a music icon as she embraced the ever-changing sounds of her surroundings.

Swabhimaan Swaabhimaan is an Indian soap opera directed by Mahesh Bhatt, and scripted by Shobha De and Vinod Ranganath. Swaabhimaan divulges the story of an attractive woman - Svetlana - who enters the lives of two families, disrupting their traditional values and relationships. Svetlana is a complex character, embodying both strength and vulnerability, which makes her both relatable and enigmatic. As she navigates the intricacies of love, ambition, and betrayal, her presence challenges the societal norms that have shaped the lives of those around her. The show beautifully intertwines themes of empowerment and self-discovery, highlighting Svetlana's journey as she strives to carve out her identity in a patriarchal society. The dynamics between her and the central characters, including the idealistic protagonist and the conservative matriarch, provide a rich tapestry of conflict and resolution. Throughout the series, viewers witness intense emotional drama, gripping storylines, and the inevitable consequences of desire and ambition. Svetlana's relationships evolve, revealing surprising alliances and rivalries that keep audiences on the edge of their seats. As each episode unfolds, secrets are revealed, and the characters must confront their pasts, leading to unexpected transformations that resonate with the audience. With its compelling narrative and strong performances, Swaabhimaan not only entertains but also sparks conversations around issues such as gender roles, love, and personal integrity, making it a thought-provoking addition to the Indian television landscape.

Arequipa Province Arequipa is a province in the Arequipa Region, Peru. Its capital, Arequipa, is Peru's third most populous province of Peru. It borders the provinces of Islay, Camaná, Caylloma, and La Unión, as well as the Moquegua Region to the south. The province is renowned for its stunning natural beauty, characterized by a mix of majestic mountains, fertile valleys, and rich cultural heritage. Arequipa itself, often referred to as the "White City" due to its unique sillar stone architecture, boasts numerous colonial-era buildings and a vibrant historical center that has been designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site. One of the highlights of the province is the majestic Misti Volcano, which rises over 5,800 meters above sea level and is a popular destination for trekkers and adventure enthusiasts. The region's diverse landscapes also include the Colca Canyon, one of the deepest canyons in the world, where visitors can witness the impressive flight of the Andean condor. Culturally, Arequipa Province offers a rich tapestry of traditions, festivals, and culinary delights that reflect its indigenous and colonial past. Local markets, such as the San Camilo market in the city, feature an array of regional products and traditional dishes, including the famous rocoto relleno and adobo arequipeño. In addition to its natural and cultural attractions, Arequipa serves as a key economic hub in southern Peru, with activities ranging from agriculture, particularly the cultivation of grapes and other crops, to mining and tourism. As a result, the province plays a vital role in the overall development of the Arequipa Region and contributes significantly to the national economy. Overall, Arequipa Province is a destination that captivates both locals and tourists, offering a blend of breathtaking landscapes, rich history, and unique traditions that continue to thrive amidst modernity.

Trelkówko Trelkówko () is a village in the administrative district of Gmina Szczytno, within the Szczytno County in the Warmian-Masurian Voivodeship of Poland. Nestled amidst picturesque landscapes, Trelkówko is characterized by its serene environment, dotted with lush greenery and tranquil water bodies. The village is home to a small but close-knit community that engages in local agriculture and craft industries. Residents of Trelkówko enjoy a quiet rural lifestyle, frequently participating in traditional practices that reflect the rich cultural heritage of the region. Local festivals, showcasing folk music, dance, and handmade crafts, draw visitors from nearby towns, creating a vibrant atmosphere that celebrates the village's history and traditions. The surrounding area offers numerous opportunities for outdoor activities such as hiking, cycling, and fishing, attracting nature lovers and adventure seekers alike. The historical architecture, including quaint wooden houses and an old church, adds to the village's charm, making it a hidden gem in the heart of Poland. As the seasons change, Trelkówko transforms, offering striking autumn foliage and a picturesque winter landscape. The community's commitment to preserving their environment and culture ensures that Trelkówko remains a lovely destination for those looking to escape the hustle and bustle of urban life.

Klondike Highway The Klondike Highway is a highway that runs from the Alaska Panhandle through the province of British Columbia and the territory of Yukon in Canada, linking the coastal town of Skagway, Alaska to Dawson City, Yukon. Its route somewhat parallels the route used by prospectors in the 1898 Klondike Gold Rush. In both British Columbia and Yukon, the highway offers breathtaking views of rugged mountain ranges, pristine rivers, and lush forests. Travelers can experience a mix of natural beauty and historical significance as they journey along the road, which showcases the region's rich heritage and its role in the late 19th-century gold rush. The highway is not just a thoroughfare; it is a testament to the adventurous spirit of those who sought fortune in the uncharted territories. Along the way, visitors can stop at various points of interest, including the historic Gold Rush Cemetery in Skagway, where many of the early prospectors found their final resting places. There are also interpretive centers that provide insights into the challenges faced by gold seekers and the Indigenous cultures that have inhabited the region for thousands of years. As you traverse the Klondike Highway, you will encounter diverse wildlife, from moose grazing by the roadside to eagles soaring overhead. The highway is particularly popular in the summer, when travelers can enjoy hiking, fishing, and camping in the area's pristine wilderness. In winter, the landscape transforms into a snow-covered wonderland, attracting winter sports enthusiasts looking to explore the snowy trails. Whether you're passing through on a road trip or planning a strategic stay to delve deeper into the history and beauty of the Yukon, the Klondike Highway promises an unforgettable adventure filled with stunning scenery and stories from the past.
